title: Learned Sparse Retrieval
concept: Neural sparse encoders that learn term weights and expansions, combining lexical precision with learned signal.

exercises.prompt: What makes an embedding 'sparse' as discussed in the last lesson?

options.text: They are high-dimensional vectors where most values are zero.
They pack information into low-dimensional, dense lists of decimals.
They ignore the vocabulary of the text entirely.

exercises.explain: Traditional sparse embeddings, like TF-IDF, represent text as high-dimensional vectors where most values are zero, and each dimension corresponds to an exact word in the vocabulary.

cards.title: Beyond Simple Matching
cards.body: Classic keyword search breaks down when people use different words for the same thing, while dense search can sometimes lose track of exact matches like product serial numbers. What if we could use AI to automatically upgrade our keywords before we search?

cards.title: Learned Sparse Retrieval
cards.body: Learned sparse retrieval uses neural networks to analyze text and predict which vocabulary words are most relevant. It automatically assigns weights to the original words and injects important related terms (expansions) to make the text searchable even if different synonyms are used.

cards.title: The Expert Tour Guide
cards.body: Think of a seasoned tour guide showing visitors around a museum. Instead of just reading the sign next to a painting, they highlight the most important details and explain related concepts that aren't written on the label, helping you connect the dots.

cards.title: Dynamic Query Expansion
cards.body: If a user searches for 'cell phone screen repair', a learned sparse encoder might amplify the weight of 'repair' and automatically inject the terms 'smartphone', 'glass', and 'fix' into the sparse vector, even if those words weren't in the original query.

cards.title: The Dense-Only Myth
cards.body: Many believe neural networks can only output dense, unreadable lists of numbers. In reality, learned sparse encoders output vectors where each dimension maps directly to a real word in the dictionary, keeping the search results explainable.

cards.title: Classic vs. Learned Search
cards.body: Comparing classic statistical sparse retrieval with learned AI-powered sparse retrieval.

left.text: Counts exact words present in the text, ignoring synonyms and context.

right.text: Learns context, weights important words, and injects missing synonyms.

exercises.prompt: What is the main benefit of 'term expansion' in learned sparse retrieval?

options.text: It compresses the document to take up less disk space.
It adds related words to the vector representation to match synonyms.
It translates the entire document into another language.
It converts all words into random numerical codes.

exercises.explain: Term expansion is the process where a neural model adds contextually relevant words (like 'automobile' for a query about 'cars') directly into the sparse representation to prevent mismatch.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Learned sparse retrieval models rely purely on basic word counts without any neural network analysis.
exercises.explain: Learned sparse retrieval uses neural networks to determine weights, unlike traditional TF-IDF which relies on simple statistics like raw word counts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phrase that describes what the model calculates for each word in the vocabulary:
exercises.sentence: A learned sparse encoder uses a neural network to calculate term weights for vocabulary words.
exercises.explain: Learned sparse encoders analyze the context of a sentence to calculate term weights for both original and expanded words, improving search precision.

exercises.prompt: Identify the output representation that represents a learned sparse retrieval model with term expansion:

options.text: A list of raw frequency counts: {'coffee': 2, 'morning': 1, 'drink': 1}

exercises.explain: Option B shows an AI-driven learned sparse representation where vocabulary terms are assigned learned weights and relevant external concepts (like 'caffeine') are automatically expanded and weighted.

exercises.prompt: How does a learned sparse retrieval model (like SPLADE) actually improve search matching compared to standard TF-IDF?

options.text: It uses a neural network to weight original terms and add contextually relevant vocabulary words.
It converts the text into a dense 768-dimension vector of floating-point numbers.
It completely ignores the original words and only searches using synonyms.
It uses manual rules and a dictionary to look up synonyms for every noun.

exercises.explain: Learned sparse retrieval uses neural networks to output a sparse vector containing weights for both the original query terms and newly added context-relevant terms (expansions) directly in the vocabulary space, giving you the best of both world-class vocabulary matching and semantic expansion.
